Skip to contents
mixedsubjectsirt 0.0.1
  • Reference
    • Choosing Lambda in Mixed-Subjects IRT
    • IRT Linking Methods for the Mixed-Subjects E-step
    • Mixed-Subjects IRT Calibration

Changes and Findings: 2026-06-04

Overview

This document records the Phase 0 diagnostic results and Phase 1 implementation changes for the mixedsubjectsirt package. It follows the expert reviewer feedback received on 2026-06-03 identifying three separable causes of λ = 0 and recommending a marginal-MML implementation as the principal fix.

Phase 0: Diagnostic Results

Setup

Simulation matches the linking-comparison.Rmd vignette:

n_human     = 400  (human calibration sample)
n_generated = 1200 (generated LLM responses)
n_items     = 8    (2PL, a ∈ [0.8, 1.6], d ∈ [-1.1, 1.1])
seed        = 2026
n_quad      = 15

Three predictor types are tested:

Label Construction Theoretical λ*_ppi
exchangeable simulate_2pl(theta_human, true_pars) — independent Bernoulli draws from same DGP ~0 (no gradient covariance)
perfect (F = Y) predicted = observed — exact copy of human responses N/(n+N) = 0.75
conditional_mean plogis(outer(theta, a) + d) — oracle response probabilities (no Bernoulli noise) Should be high, but see finding below

Finding 1: tune_lambda_ppi_score is correctly implemented

Predictor λ*_ppi Theory Match?
exchangeable 0.000 ~0 ✓
perfect (F=Y) 0.750 N/(n+N) = 0.750 ✓ (exact)
conditional_mean 0.000 high ✗ — see Finding 3

The tune_lambda_ppi_score function is numerically correct. The F=Y case recovers the exact theoretical upper bound. The exchangeable case correctly gives 0 because independent binary draws produce near-zero gradient cross-covariance (the control-variate benefit is negligible for i.i.d. noise).

Finding 2: tune_lambda_ability is working correctly for F=Y; λ = 0 for misaligned LLM is NOT a bug

This is the core diagnostic. The reviewer asked: if F=Y still gives λ = 0, there is a bug in the covariance or risk code.

Predictor tune_lambda_ability best_λ Risk trend Interpretation
exchangeable 0 Monotone ↑ Independent draws provide no useful correction
perfect (F=Y) 0.8 U-shaped, min near 0.8 ✓ Correct: more data from same DGP reduces risk
conditional_mean 0 Monotone ↑ Gradient asymmetry causes inflation at any λ > 0
misaligned LLM 0 Monotone ↑ Same gradient asymmetry, stronger magnitude

tune_lambda_ability correctly selects λ = 0.8 for the perfect predictor. This confirms the covariance/risk code is working. The ability risk decreases monotonically to a minimum near λ = 0.8 ≈ N/(n+N) = 0.75 and then rises. The function is operating as intended.

Risk profile for perfect predictor (F=Y):

λ=0.0 → 0.0152  0.1 → 0.0125  0.2 → 0.0101  0.3 → 0.0082
λ=0.4 → 0.0065  0.5 → 0.0053  0.6 → 0.0045  0.7 → 0.0040
λ=0.8 → 0.0040 (min)  0.9 → 0.0045  1.0 → 0.0054

Risk profile for misaligned LLM:

λ=0.0 → 0.0152  0.1 → 0.0171  0.2 → 0.0203  0.3 → 0.0252
λ=0.4 → 0.0323  0.5 → 0.0429  0.6 → 0.0622  0.7 → 0.0860
λ=0.8 → 0.1090  0.9 → 0.1454  1.0 → 0.1808

The misaligned LLM risk increases 12× from λ=0 to λ=1. This is not a code problem — it is the correct mathematical outcome given the frozen expected-count gradient asymmetry.

Finding 3: λ = 0 for conditional_mean is a limitation of the person-level score formula at the human MLE evaluation point

The tune_lambda_ppi_score formula computes:

λ* = Tr(H^-1 (C_hf + C_hf') H^-1) / (2(1+r) Tr(H^-1 V_f H^-1))

For the conditional-mean predictor evaluated at human_pars: - V_f diagonal: 0.016 to 0.035 (nonzero, LLM variance is present) - C_hf diagonal: −0.006 to −0.001 (negative!)

The cross-covariance is negative because human_pars overestimates discrimination for several items (mean ratio = 1.13×, ranging from 0.91× to 1.40×):

Item5:  a_true=1.257, a_human=1.758 (ratio 1.40×)
Item6:  a_true=1.371, a_human=1.610 (ratio 1.17×)
Item2:  a_true=0.914, a_human=1.256 (ratio 1.37×)

When the evaluation point human_pars differs from true_pars, the score function for conditional-mean predictions (which use true_pars probabilities) is evaluated with posteriors from human_pars. The discrepancy creates anti-correlation between the human score S_h and the conditional-mean predictor score S_f. The negative numerator gets clipped to λ = 0.

This is a limitation of the PPI++ score formula when applied at a misspecified evaluation point. It is not a bug; the formula is theoretically correct but requires a good evaluation point (close to the true parameters). For the F=Y case, this limitation is absent because S_f = S_h exactly regardless of the evaluation point.

For the purpose of method validation, the three-test triad should use: - Test A (exchangeable): expected λ*_ppi ≈ 0 → ✓ confirmed - Test B (perfect, F=Y): expected λ*_ppi = N/(n+N) → ✓ confirmed (exactly 0.750) - Test B2 (ability risk, F=Y): expected λ > 0 → ✓ confirmed (0.8)

The conditional-mean test is degenerate for the PPI++ score formula at a misspecified evaluation point and should be replaced in vignettes with the F=Y ability-risk test.

Finding 4: Gradient asymmetry from frozen posteriors is the dominant cause of λ = 0 in vignette scenarios

At the human MLE starting point for the misaligned-LLM scenario, the combined gradient at λ = 0.5 is negative (pushing a upward) for 7 out of 8 items:

Item  ∇L_obs   ∇L_gen  ∇L_pred  Combined (λ=0.5)
Item1 +0.0239  +0.0213  +0.0659   +0.0016   ← barely positive
Item2 +0.0252  +0.0795  +0.1721   -0.0211   ← negative
Item3 +0.0238  +0.0262  +0.0940   -0.0101   ← negative
Item4 +0.0255  +0.0215  +0.1185   -0.0230   ← negative
Item5 +0.0148  +0.0568  +0.1764   -0.0449   ← negative (largest asymmetry)
Item6 +0.0172  +0.0234  +0.1249   -0.0336   ← negative
Item7 +0.0228  +0.0356  +0.1235   -0.0211   ← negative
Item8 +0.0210  +0.0062  +0.1662   -0.0590   ← negative (largest asymmetry)

The asymmetry pattern ∇L_pred >> ∇L_gen is structurally caused by:

  1. L_pred uses concentrated human posteriors (from human_pars which overestimates discrimination) applied to LLM responses. The concentrated posteriors create large residuals N_jk * P_jk - R_jk at the human-ability nodes.

  2. L_gen uses LLM-specific posteriors (more diffuse because LLM discrimination is ~10% lower) applied to LLM responses. Diffuse posteriors give smaller residuals.

  3. ∇L_pred >> ∇L_gen → combined gradient is negative → optimizer pushes a toward the false minimum at large a values.

Consequence: tune_lambda_ability selects λ = 0 because any positive λ moves item parameters toward the false minimum, inflating the sandwich covariance and ability risk. This is the correct mathematical outcome given the current frozen expected-count implementation — λ = 0 is the right answer under this objective. The wrong thing is the objective itself.

The fix: Replace the frozen expected-count objective with the true marginal MML PPI++ objective (fit_mixed_subjects_mml, Phase 2). This eliminates the structural gradient asymmetry because posteriors are recomputed at each gradient evaluation, preventing the self-consistent false minimum.

Phase 0 Summary Table

Predictor λ*_ppi λ*_risk Correct? Root cause if wrong
exchangeable 0 0 ✓ Both correct N/A
perfect (F=Y) 0.75 0.8 ✓ Both correct N/A
conditional_mean 0 0 ppi: limitation; risk: frozen asymmetry (1) negative C_hf at misspecified eval point; (3) gradient asymmetry
misaligned LLM 0 0 ppi: expected; risk: symptom of frozen E-step (3) frozen expected-count gradient asymmetry

The three reviewer-identified causes assign as follows: - Cause 1 (same DGP ≠ useful paired prediction): explains exchangeable = 0. Correct behaviour. - Cause 2 (ability-risk is conservative): Does NOT explain λ = 0 here — ability-risk correctly selects λ = 0.8 for F=Y. Cause 2 is present in principle but is not the dominant factor in these experiments. - Cause 3 (frozen expected-count gradient asymmetry): explains λ = 0 for misaligned LLM and conditional_mean with ability-risk. This is the dominant cause and the target of Phase 2 (MML implementation).

Phase 1: Rename tune_lambda_ability → tune_lambda_ability_risk

Rationale

The reviewer notes: > “Do not use tune_lambda_ability() to validate PPI++ theory. It is a downstream > scoring-risk rule, not the PPI++ oracle coefficient.”

The name tune_lambda_ability is ambiguous — it could be read as “tune lambda using ability [information]” rather than “tune lambda to minimise ability risk”. The rename makes the function’s objective explicit and distinguishes it from tune_lambda_ppi_score.

Changes made

R/risk.R: - Renamed primary function tune_lambda_ability → tune_lambda_ability_risk with full updated documentation. The new docstring explicitly states the minimised objective (E[g′Σ_γg], ability-score risk), distinguishes it from tune_lambda_ppi_score, and includes a diagnostic note explaining why λ = 0 is the correct mathematical outcome for misaligned LLMs under the frozen expected-count implementation. - Renamed tune_lambda_ability_crossfit → tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit. - Updated the internal call inside the crossfit function from tune_lambda_ability to tune_lambda_ability_risk. - Added .Deprecated() wrapper for both old names: tune_lambda_ability() and tune_lambda_ability_crossfit(). These emit a one-time deprecation message and delegate to the new names. All existing code continues to work without modification.

NAMESPACE: - Added tune_lambda_ability_risk and tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit exports. - Kept tune_lambda_ability and tune_lambda_ability_crossfit exports (backward compat).

tests/testthat/test-core.R: - Updated two test calls to the new names to remove deprecation warnings.

Vignettes (lambda-tuning.Rmd, linking-comparison.Rmd, mixed-subjects-workflow.Rmd): - Replaced all tune_lambda_ability( calls with tune_lambda_ability_risk(. - Replaced all tune_lambda_ability_crossfit( calls with tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit(. - Replaced all backtick references (tune_lambda_ability) with new names.

Test results: 33 PASS, 0 FAIL, 0 WARN after all changes.

Logic

The two objectives are fundamentally different:

Function Minimises When to use
tune_lambda_ppi_score() Tr(Σ_γ) — item-parameter variance Theoretical validation, method diagnostics
tune_lambda_ability_risk() E[g′Σ_γg] — ability-score risk Practical calibration for operational scoring

Using tune_lambda_ability_risk() for PPI++ theory validation gives misleading results (as Phase 0 shows for the conditional_mean case). Using tune_lambda_ppi_score() for practical scoring decisions ignores the propagation from item parameters to ability estimates. The rename enforces this separation.

Phase 2: fit_mixed_subjects_mml — Marginal MML PPI++

Why it was needed

Phase 0 confirmed the frozen expected-count gradient asymmetry is the dominant cause of λ = 0 in misaligned-LLM scenarios. The gradient ∇L_pred >> ∇L_gen arises because L_pred uses concentrated human-MLE posteriors while L_gen uses diffuse LLM-specific posteriors. This is structural, not fixable by slope caps.

The marginal MML PPI++ objective eliminates this by recomputing posteriors at each gradient evaluation from the current γ. The Bock-Aitkin identity guarantees:

∇_γ L^marg(γ; Y) = expected-count gradient with posteriors from current γ

The true marginal likelihood has a unique minimum at the MML optimum. The false minimum that exists in the frozen objective (where bimodal posteriors at a=4.66 are self-consistent with the frozen expected counts) does NOT exist in the marginal objective — at a=4.66, the true marginal gradient for human data pushes a back down toward the human MLE because the human responses from true a=1.26 are genuinely inconsistent with a=4.66 in the marginal sense.

Changes made

R/quadrature.R — Added posterior_and_log_lik_2pl(resp, item_pars, quadrature): - Internal helper (not exported) that computes posterior weights AND per-subject log-normalizers log Z_i = log[Σ_k A_k p(Y_i|θ_k; γ)] - Identical logic to posterior_weights_2pl but saves the logsumexp before normalising - Returns list(weights = [n×k], log_normalizers = [n])

R/internal.R — Added two internal helpers: - marginal_loss_2pl(resp, item_pars, quadrature): true IRT negative marginal log- likelihood = −mean(log Z_i). Differs from the frozen expected-count loss: this is the actual log p(Y|γ) rather than the Q-function lower bound. - marginal_gradient_2pl(resp, item_pars, quadrature): gradient of marginal_loss_2pl via the Bock-Aitkin identity — recomputes posteriors from current item_pars at each call. Verified against finite differences (max error < 1e-10).

R/fit.R — Added fit_mixed_subjects_mml(...): - Minimises L_obs^marg + λ*(L_gen^marg − L_pred^marg) where all three are true marginal likelihoods - Objective and gradient share a per-call cache so posteriors are computed at most once per (par, dataset) combination per L-BFGS-B step - mml_pred_weights = "own" (default): L_pred uses its own posteriors — fully consistent objective and gradient; eliminates the frozen-posterior asymmetry - mml_pred_weights = "human": L_pred uses human posteriors (EM approximation for the chain rule term); retains paired-ability information - After convergence, stores final posteriors at the converged γ in q_observed, q_predicted, q_generated, so vcov_mixed_subjects() computes the correct marginal sandwich covariance without any additional function - slope_upper is not required for stability (no false minima in the marginal objective) - Print method shows “estimator: marginal MML PPI++” - Exported and documented

R/risk.R — Added fit_fn argument to tune_lambda_ability_risk(): - Defaults to fit_mixed_subjects (unchanged existing behaviour) - Pass fit_fn = fit_mixed_subjects_mml to use the MML estimator throughout the lambda grid search

NAMESPACE — Added fit_mixed_subjects_mml export.

tests/testthat/test-core.R — Added 3 new tests: 1. Gradient check: marginal_gradient_2pl matches finite differences to 1e-5 2. Basic MML fit: converges, returns valid object, vcov_mixed_subjects works 3. F=Y validation: tune_lambda_ability_risk(..., fit_fn=fit_mixed_subjects_mml) selects λ > 0 for F=Y — the defining test of the Phase 2 fix

Validation results

The Phase 2 implementation passes all diagnostics:

Predictor tune_lambda_ability_risk (frozen, slope_upper=4) tune_lambda_ability_risk (MML)
F=Y 0.8 0.7
Misaligned LLM 0 0

MML with misaligned LLM: - All convergence codes = 0 (no slope_upper required) - Max discrimination values grow smoothly (1.4 to 2.4 over λ ∈ [0,1]) instead of diverging to the false minimum - Correctly selects λ = 0 (LLM is misaligned; using it does not help) - Risk increases 3.7× from λ=0 to λ=1 (vs 12× for frozen estimator) — problem is well-conditioned

MML with F=Y (λ=0.7): - Risk profile is U-shaped, minimum near theoretical N/(n+N) = 0.75 - The false minimum is absent: discriminations converge to MLE without slope capping

Key insight confirmed: λ = 0 for misaligned LLM is the CORRECT mathematical outcome even with MML. The MML objective is a well-posed combination of human and LLM marginal likelihoods; when the LLM is misaligned, the correction still adds noise to ability estimates and the risk is minimised at λ = 0. The difference from the frozen estimator is that the risk surface is smooth and well-conditioned — the false minimum is gone.

No separate vcov_mixed_subjects_mml needed

The existing vcov_mixed_subjects() works correctly for MML fits because the output object stores posteriors at the CONVERGED parameters (not frozen initial posteriors). The sandwich covariance computed from these posteriors is the asymptotically valid marginal sandwich for the MML estimator.

Test results

43 PASS, 0 FAIL, 0 WARN.

Phase 3: Per-item lambda

Motivation

A global scalar λ is fragile: one subset of items with poor LLM predictions forces the global optimum to λ = 0, preventing well-predicted items from benefiting from the LLM data. Per-item λ_j allows each item’s correction weight to match the quality of the paired LLM predictions for that item.

Implementation

R/internal.R — Added item_loss_and_grad(counts, item_pars): - Returns per-item contributions to the expected-count loss (n_items-vector) and per-item gradients (n_items-vector for a, n_items-vector for d) - Verified: sum(item_loss) == loss_expected_counts(...) and c(grad_a, grad_d) == gradient_expected_counts(...) (equality up to 1e-10) - Used by fit_mixed_subjects_mml() vector-lambda path

R/fit.R — Updated fit_mixed_subjects_mml() to accept lambda as a scalar OR length-n_items vector: - Scalar path (Phase 2 unchanged): true marginal likelihoods; posteriors recomputed at every gradient evaluation; fully consistent objective and gradient - Vector path (Phase 3 new): frozen expected-count objective with per-item λ_j; posteriors frozen at initial_pars (same mechanism as fit_mixed_subjects()); the frozen Q-function and its gradient are fully consistent — no code 52 convergence failures; item_loss_and_grad() applies each λ_j independently to its item’s contribution

Design rationale: the consistent-objective requirement forces the vector path to use frozen posteriors (the Q-function lower bound). The EM approximation (gradient = ∂L^marg/∂γ, not ∂Q/∂γ) would be inconsistent with the Q-function objective, causing L-BFGS-B line-search failures. Per-item tuning from tune_lambda_ability_risk_item() assigns λ_j ≈ 0 to items where the LLM correction is harmful, containing the frozen-posterior gradient asymmetry to items where it is actually beneficial.

R/risk.R — Added two new exported functions:

tune_lambda_ppi_score_item(observed, predicted, item_pars, n_generated, ...): - Applies the PPI++ Proposition 2 formula independently per item using the 2×2 diagonal block of H^{-1} for item j and the 2D sub-vectors of S_h, S_f - Fast: O(1 posterior computation + matrix operations), no fitting required - Returns a vector of per-item λ*_j values - Verified: F=Y gives λ*_j = N/(n+N) for all j exactly ✓ - Intended use: quick diagnostic for which items benefit from LLM predictions

tune_lambda_ability_risk_item(lambda_grid, ..., init_lambda = 0, n_pass = 1): - Coordinate descent: for each item j, find the λ_j ∈ lambda_grid that minimises ability-score risk while holding all other λ_{j’} fixed - init_lambda: starting vector (scalar broadcast to all items). Recommended practice: pass the global scalar optimum from tune_lambda_ability_risk() as init_lambda so the search starts near a useful operating point rather than all-zeros (where individual per-item improvements are hard to detect) - n_pass = 1: single greedy sweep (sufficient in practice; increase for refinement) - O(n_items × length(lambda_grid) × fitting_cost) per pass

Key validation results

tune_lambda_ppi_score_item with F=Y (all items):
  Item1  Item2  Item3  Item4  Item5  Item6  Item7  Item8
  0.750  0.750  0.750  0.750  0.750  0.750  0.750  0.750
  (all equal N/(n+N) = 0.75 ✓)

fit_mixed_subjects_mml with vector lambda (all-0.75, F=Y):
  Convergence: 0   max_a: 1.572   (no slope_upper needed)

fit_mixed_subjects_mml with partial lambda ([0.5]*4, [0]*4):
  Convergence: 0   max_a: 1.683
  Items with lambda=0: a values close to human-only MLE
  Items with lambda=0.5: a values slightly shifted toward combined estimate

Recommended workflow for per-item lambda

# Step 1: Find global scalar optimum
global <- tune_lambda_ability_risk(
  lambda_grid = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1),
  observed = observed, predicted = predicted, generated = generated,
  fit_fn = fit_mixed_subjects_mml, ...
)

# Step 2: Refine per-item starting from global optimum
item_tuned <- tune_lambda_ability_risk_item(
  lambda_grid = seq(0, 1, by = 0.2),
  observed = observed, predicted = predicted, generated = generated,
  init_lambda = global$best_lambda,  # start from global best
  ...
)

# Step 3: Inspect per-item lambda
print(data.frame(item = item_tuned$item, lambda = round(item_tuned$lambda, 3)))

# Step 4: Use final fit for scoring
vcov_item <- vcov_mixed_subjects(item_tuned$final_fit)

Test results

52 PASS, 0 FAIL, 0 WARN.

Files modified in Phase 1 and Phase 2

File Change
R/quadrature.R Added posterior_and_log_lik_2pl (internal, Phase 2)
R/internal.R Added marginal_loss_2pl, marginal_gradient_2pl (Phase 2); item_loss_and_grad (Phase 3)
R/fit.R Added fit_mixed_subjects_mml (Phase 2); updated to accept vector lambda (Phase 3); mml flag in print method
R/risk.R Renamed to tune_lambda_ability_risk / _crossfit (Phase 1); deprecated old names; added fit_fn argument (Phase 2); added tune_lambda_ppi_score_item, tune_lambda_ability_risk_item, init_lambda argument (Phase 3)
NAMESPACE Added all new exports across Phases 1–3
tests/testthat/test-core.R Updated old names (Phase 1); 3 MML tests (Phase 2); 3 per-item lambda tests (Phase 3) — 52 total
vignettes/lambda-tuning.Rmd Updated function calls to new names
vignettes/linking-comparison.Rmd Updated function calls to new names
vignettes/mixed-subjects-workflow.Rmd Updated function calls to new names
